lineage: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
Eusebius, Bishop of Nicomedia, and his Party, by again endeavoring to introduce the Arian Heresy, create Disturbances in the Churches.

ccel.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

title: Eusebius, Bishop of Nicomedia, and his Party, by again endeavoring to introduce the Arian Heresy, create Disturbances in the Churches.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: After the death of the Emperor Constantine, Eusebius, bishop of Nicomedia, and Theognis of Nicæa, imagining that a favorable opportunity had arisen, used their utmost efforts to expunge the doctrine of homoousion, and to introduce Arianism in its place.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: They, nevertheless, despaired of effecting this, if Athanasius should return to Alexandria: in order therefore to accomplish their designs, they sought the assistance of that presbyter by whose means Arius had been recalled from exile a little before.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: How this was done shall now be described.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The presbyter in question presented the will and the request of the deceased king to his son Constantius; who finding those dispositions in it which he was most desirous of, for the empire of the East was by his father’s will apportioned to him, treated the presbyter with great consideration, loaded him with favors, and ordered that free access should be given him both to the palace and to himself.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This license soon obtained for him familiar intercourse with the empress, as well as with her eunuchs.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: There was at that time a chief eunuch of the imperial bed-chamber named Eusebius; him the presbyter persuaded to adopt Arian’s views, after which the rest of the eunuchs were also prevailed on to adopt the same sentiments.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Not only this but the empress also, under the influence of the eunuchs and the presbyters, became favorable to the tenets of Arius; and not long after the subject was introduced to the emperor himself.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Thus it became gradually diffused throughout the court, and among the officers of the imperial household and guards, until at length it spread itself over the whole population of the city.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The chamberlains in the palace discussed this doctrine with the women; and in the family of every citizen there was a logical contest.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Moreover, the mischief quickly extended to other provinces and cities, the controversy, like a spark, insignificant at first, exciting in the auditors a spirit of contention: for every one who inquired the cause of the tumult, found immediately occasion for disputing, and determined to take part in the strife at the moment of making the inquiry.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: By general altercation of this kind all order was subverted; the agitation, however, was confined to the cities of the East, those of Illyricum and the western parts of the empire meanwhile were perfectly tranquil, because they would not annul the decisions of the Council of Nicæa.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: As this affair increased, going from bad to worse, Eusebius of Nicomedia and his party looked upon popular ferment as a piece of good fortune.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For only thus they thought they would be enabled to constitute some one who held their own sentiments bishop of Alexandria.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But the return of Athanasius at that time defeated their purpose; for he came thither fortified by a letter from one of the Augusti, which the younger Constantine, who bore his father’s name, addressed to the people of Alexandria, from Treves, a city in Gaul.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: A copy of this epistle is here subjoined.
